basics.name: Carlos A. Leyva-Capote
basics.label: Researcher
basics.summary: Applied Mathematics M.S. student at UPRM researching computational cognitive modeling, neural-symbolic AI, and natural language processing.

work.name: YouTube (Google)
highlights: Engineered analysis pipelines to evaluate video upscaling algorithms and ML models using Universal Video Quality (UVQ) metrics, informing bitrate optimization decisions for YouTube Shorts.

work.name: Google Devices & Services - Pixel
work.position: Software Engineering Intern - Spatial Audio Team
highlights: Delivered a proof-of-concept for a 50KB multimodal enhancement model for speech recognition, leading to $1M savings in data acquisition for retraining a 50MB model.
Prototyped a 6DOF spatial audio system with virtual assistant integration for enhanced environmental interaction (Unity, C#) and presented to upper-level management.

work.name: Google Cloud Platform
highlights: Delivered end-to-end NLU feature for proactive assistive suggestions in Google Workspace.

work.name: Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory (JHU APL)
work.position: AI Researcher, AI, Neuroscience, and Complex Systems Group
highlights: Developing a multimodal speech-decoding framework fusing video, audio, and neural signals to support assistive communication for speakers with dysarthria.
Conducted modality ablation studies, improving Phoneme Error Rate by 12% and WER by 11% over baseline.
Architected an agentic laboratory system using Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to orchestrate visual inspection and automate experimental decision-making.

work.name: Climate TRACE
highlights: Developed a hierarchical Bayesian model to infer vehicle fleet composition for regions lacking reliable statistics, directly supporting global emissions estimation.
Designed a stratified sampling workflow for data augmentation using computer vision models.

work.name: MIT Lincoln Laboratory
work.position: Research Co-op, AI Technology and Systems Group
highlights: Designed data augmentation architecture using knowledge graphs for few-shot authorship verification.
Achieved 92% obfuscation and 78% impersonation success rates via LLM-based adversarial attacks.

work.name: National Science Foundation - Computational Linguistics Group
highlights: Developing a cognitively plausible neural-symbolic parser for syntactic disambiguation.

work.name: National Science Foundation LREU
work.position: Undergraduate Research Fellow - Hybrid Paradigms in AI
highlights: Synthesized literature on symbolic vs. connectionist approaches to analyze the divergence between LLM reasoning and human cognitive processes in structured tasks.

work.name: Computing Alliance for Hispanic-Serving Institutions
work.position: AI Research - Automatic Dysarthric Speech Recognition
highlights: Fine-tuned ASR models via supervised teacher-student distillation, achieving 48% WER for extreme dysarthria.
Focus: Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Numerical Analysis
Specialization: Curricular Sequence in Cognitive Science

awards.title: First Place, Computing Presentation
awards.summary: Awarded for: "Towards Automatic Dysarthric Speech Recognition"

publications.name: Masks and Mimicry: Strategic Obfuscation and Impersonation Attacks on Authorship Verification
A Cognitively Plausible Neural-Symbolic Model for Syntactic Disambiguation in Minimalist Grammar
